mappings.rationaleAI: Encompasses simple machines (B.1) through complex mechanical systems with gears, cams, linkages (B.3), and power transmission mechanisms (B.2), directly corresponding to machines and machine components.
The wheel and axle as a classical simple machine (B.1), with additional functions as potter's wheel and flywheel relating to machine components.
Covers prehistoric development of stone, bone, and wood tools (A.1), the role of metalworking in tool development (A.1), and basic types of hand tools extending through industrialization (A.2).
The six classical mechanical devices — lever, wheel and axle, pulley, inclined plane, wedge, and screw — directly correspond to simple machines (B.1).
One of humanity's oldest tools evolving from stone to steel blades (A.1), classified as a cutting tool (A.2.b).
A rigid beam pivoting on a fulcrum, the simplest of the six classical simple machines (B.1), with three classes distinguished by fulcrum, effort, and load positions.
Design, construction, and operation of robots in manufacturing applications corresponds to robots in computer-aided machining (D.3).
Programmable machines executing operations via stored programs correspond to computer numerical control (D.1) and CAD/CAM systems (D.2) in computer-aided machining.
The development of machine tools, steam-powered mechanized production, and iron metallurgy corresponds to the history and characteristics of machine tools (C.1).
Established foundational principles of statics (law of the lever) and designed compound pulleys and the Archimedean screw, corresponding to simple machines (B.1).
Traces tool-making from Stone Age lithics through metallurgy to modern manufacturing, corresponding to early history of hand tools (A.1) and history of machine tools (C.1).
Paleolithic and Neolithic lithic tool use corresponds to early history of hand tools including stone tools (A.1).
Iron as a material for tool development from the Iron Age onward corresponds to the development of metal tools (A.1).
Design, analysis, and manufacture of mechanical systems directly relate to machines and machine components (B), machine tools (C), and computer-aided machining systems (D).
The science of metals underpins the development of metal tools (A.1) and the operation of metal-cutting tools (C.2).
Transformation of raw materials into finished goods using machines and tools corresponds to machine tools (C) and computer-aided manufacturing (D).
Bronze smelting enabling superior tools corresponds to the development of metal tools in the early history of hand tools (A.1).
Stone tool use from 3.3 million years ago through the Neolithic corresponds to Paleolithic and Neolithic stone tools (A.1).
Force as the vector quantity changing velocity or shape, with mechanical advantage as output-to-input force ratio, underpins the physics of simple machines (B.1).
Applied engineering practice using science and design processes connects to machine tools (C) and computer-aided design and manufacturing (D).
